questions.q: What does the symbol 'n' represent in nuclear reactions?
opts: (A) Proton
(B) Neutron
(C) Electron
(D) Alpha particle
questions.explanation: Neutrons are represented by 'n'. They are subatomic particles with no charge.

questions.q: Which of the following nuclear reactions is an example of beta decay?
opts: (A) fission
(B) fusion
(C) electron capture
(D) alpha decay
questions.explanation: Beta decay is a type of radioactive decay where an electron is emitted from the nucleus.

questions.q: What is the mass defect in a nucleus?
opts: (A) The difference between the actual mass and the sum of the masses of its constituent protons and neutrons
(B) The sum of the masses of the protons and neutrons in a nucleus
(C) The difference between the sum of the masses of its constituent protons and neutrons and the actual mass of the nucleus
(D) The mass of a single proton
questions.explanation: Mass defect is the difference between the actual mass and the sum of the masses of its constituent protons and neutrons.

questions.q: What is the ratio of the number of neutrons to protons in a stable nucleus called?
opts: (A) Proton number
(B) Neutron number
(C) Mass number
(D) Nucleon ratio
questions.explanation: The ratio of the number of neutrons to protons in a stable nucleus is called the nucleon ratio.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a radioactive decay series?
opts: (A) Alpha decay
(B) Beta decay
(C) Gamma decay
(D) Uranium-238 to Lead-206
questions.explanation: A radioactive decay series is a series of radioactive decays that occur from the parent nucleus to a stable nucleus.

questions.q: What is the binding energy of a nucleus?
opts: (A) The energy required to disintegrate a nucleus
(B) The energy released when a nucleus is formed
(C) The energy required to remove a nucleon from a nucleus
(D) The energy required to remove a nucleon from a nucleus and place it at infinity
questions.explanation: The binding energy of a nucleus is the energy required to remove a nucleon from a nucleus and place it at infinity.

questions.q: In a nuclear reaction, what is the law of conservation of energy?
opts: (A) The energy of the reactants is greater than the energy of the products
(B) The energy of the products is greater than the energy of the reactants
(C) The energy of the reactants is equal to the energy of the products
(D) The energy of the reactants is not equal to the energy of the products
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the energy of the reactants is equal to the energy of the products in a nuclear reaction.

questions.q: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
opts: (A) The time taken for the activity of a substance to decrease to half its initial value
(B) The time taken for the activity of a substance to increase to double its initial value
(C) The time taken for the activity of a substance to remain constant
(D) The time taken for the activity of a substance to increase to four times its initial value
questions.explanation: The half-life of a radioactive substance is the time taken for the activity of a substance to decrease to half its initial value.

questions.q: In a nuclear reaction, what is the law of conservation of momentum?
opts: (A) The momentum of the reactants is greater than the momentum of the products
(B) The momentum of the products is greater than the momentum of the reactants
(C) The momentum of the reactants is equal to the momentum of the products
(D) The momentum of the reactants is not equal to the momentum of the products
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of momentum states that the momentum of the reactants is equal to the momentum of the products in a nuclear reaction.

questions.q: What is the difference between alpha and beta decay?
opts: (A) Alpha decay is a type of beta decay
(B) Beta decay is a type of alpha decay
(C) Alpha decay is the emission of an alpha particle, while beta decay is the emission of a beta particle
(D) Alpha decay is the emission of a beta particle, while beta decay is the emission of an alpha particle
questions.explanation: Alpha decay is the emission of an alpha particle, while beta decay is the emission of a beta particle.

questions.q: What is the mass number of an atom?
opts: (A) The sum of the number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus
(B) The number of protons in the nucleus
(C) The number of neutrons in the nucleus
(D) The number of electrons in the atom
questions.explanation: The mass number of an atom is the sum of the number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus.

questions.q: In a nuclear reaction, what is the law of conservation of energy-momentum?
opts: (A) The energy-momentum of the reactants is greater than the energy-momentum of the products
(B) The energy-momentum of the products is greater than the energy-momentum of the reactants
(C) The energy-momentum of the reactants is equal to the energy-momentum of the products
(D) The energy-momentum of the reactants is not equal to the energy-momentum of the products
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy-momentum states that the energy-momentum of the reactants is equal to the energy-momentum of the products in a nuclear reaction.

questions.q: What is the binding energy per nucleon in a nucleus?
opts: (A) The energy required to disintegrate a nucleus
(B) The energy released when a nucleus is formed
(C) The energy required to remove a nucleon from a nucleus
(D) The energy required to remove a nucleon from a nucleus and place it at infinity
questions.explanation: The binding energy per nucleon is the energy required to remove a nucleon from a nucleus and place it at infinity.

questions.q: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance used for?
opts: (A) To determine the activity of a substance
(B) To determine the mass number of a substance
(C) To determine the half-life of a substance
(D) To determine the binding energy of a substance
questions.explanation: The half-life of a radioactive substance is used to determine the half-life of a substance.

questions.q: In a nuclear reaction, what is the law of conservation of charge?
opts: (A) The charge of the reactants is greater than the charge of the products
(B) The charge of the products is greater than the charge of the reactants
(C) The charge of the reactants is equal to the charge of the products
(D) The charge of the reactants is not equal to the charge of the products
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of charge states that the charge of the reactants is equal to the charge of the products in a nuclear reaction.

questions.q: What is the difference between a nuclear reaction and a chemical reaction?
opts: (A) A nuclear reaction involves the emission of radiation, while a chemical reaction does not
(B) A chemical reaction involves the emission of radiation, while a nuclear reaction does not
(C) A nuclear reaction involves the emission of electrons, while a chemical reaction involves the emission of radiation
(D) A nuclear reaction involves the emission of radiation, while a chemical reaction involves the emission of electrons
questions.explanation: A nuclear reaction involves the emission of radiation, while a chemical reaction does not.

questions.q: What is the mass of a neutron?
questions.explanation: The mass of a neutron is 1.008665 u.

questions.q: In a nuclear reaction, what is the law of conservation of mass-energy?
opts: (A) The mass-energy of the reactants is greater than the mass-energy of the products
(B) The mass-energy of the products is greater than the mass-energy of the reactants
(C) The mass-energy of the reactants is equal to the mass-energy of the products
(D) The mass-energy of the reactants is not equal to the mass-energy of the products
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of mass-energy states that the mass-energy of the reactants is equal to the mass-energy of the products in a nuclear reaction.
